caveats: a: BLS occupation data cover broader employment services and are heavily affected by temporary-help workers on agency payrolls.
a: The public-company workflow may not represent boutique lower-middle-market firms.
a: Task exposure is not the same as end-to-end job replacement, especially for confidential executive assessment and persuasion.
rho: SHRM respondents are employer HR professionals, not executive-search firms.
rho: Reported time savings do not quantify the share of exposed labor actually removed or redeployed.
rho: Client confidentiality, algorithmic-bias controls, and fragmented legacy databases may slow smaller firms.
e: No source measures eligibility within the injected lower-middle-market firm count.
e: A nominally qualifying firm may still be economically nontransferable if client relationships belong personally to one rainmaker.
e: The injected count is margin-bridged and may misclassify firms with unusually variable consultant compensation or owner distributions.
s5: The Gallup statistic is an all-industry intention measure, not completed control transfers.
s5: Heidrick & Struggles is far larger than the screened firms.
s5: Team lift-outs and earn-in arrangements may not qualify as control transfers of durable operating firms.
q: The observed billing structure comes from one large global retained-search firm.
q: No source measures sharing of AI productivity gains or the revenue mix of lower-middle-market firms.
q: Contingency-heavy firms may experience different retention and volume effects from retained firms.
d5: BLS publishes output for broader NAICS 561300, not executive search separately.
d5: The recent public-company revenue increase was primarily price-driven and is not a constant-quality volume measure.
d5: Executive-search demand is highly cyclical and concentrated in discretionary senior hiring.
o: No direct source measures year-five operator necessity for executive search.
o: Internal talent teams may adopt the same sourcing tools as search firms and remove routine mandates.
o: Legal, privacy, and bias rules could preserve accountable intermediaries but also increase their compliance cost.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers engage firms for senior-leadership searches. Revenue is commonly a fixed retainer linked to estimated first-year candidate compensation, sometimes supplemented by completion-related adjustments, reimbursed expenses, or contingency fees; repeat work depends on client relationships, sector specialization, and successful placements.
lens.excluded_activities: Internal corporate recruiting teams, general employment placement, temporary staffing, professional employer organizations, stand-alone human-resources consulting, coaching without a client-sponsored search, shells, and nontransferable solo practices.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market executive-search firms providing recurring or repeat retained, exclusive, or contingency recruitment and placement services for executive and senior-management roles to external business, nonprofit, and public-sector clients.

a.bridge: The exact NAICS definition identifies search-strategy development, candidate research, identification, screening, qualification verification, interviewing, negotiation, and assimilation, while broader NAICS 561300 occupation data report management, human-resources, sales, computer, and administrative work but are dominated by temporary-help employees outside 561312. I therefore weight the exact executive-search workflow and public-firm description more heavily than the broader occupation shares, treating research, database work, outreach drafting, screening support, market mapping, scheduling, and documentation as highly exposed, and discounting relationship origination, confidential persuasion, nuanced interviewing, reference judgment, negotiation, and final accountability. The broader occupational table likely understates executive-search desk-work exposure because temporary-help payroll adds many client-site occupations, while task-based judgment may overstate exposure if proprietary networks and tacit assessment cannot be codified.
caveats: BLS occupation data cover broader employment services and are heavily affected by temporary-help workers on agency payrolls.
The public-company workflow may not represent boutique lower-middle-market firms.
Task exposure is not the same as end-to-end job replacement, especially for confidential executive assessment and persuasion.
a.change_evidence: Exact 561312 payroll by occupation, time-and-motion studies from retained-search firms, and audited task-level hours before and after AI deployment for researchers, associates, consultants, and coordinators would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Executive search is an information-intensive advisory workflow with substantial labor in candidate discovery, synthesis, outreach, screening, coordination, and reporting. AI can accelerate most preparatory and administrative tasks, but senior-hire assessment and client trust remain labor-intensive and reputationally sensitive.

d5.bridge: BLS projects broader employment-services real output to grow 2.5% annually over the decade, while Heidrick & Struggles reported 4.9% executive-search net-revenue growth in 2024 but only 0.1% growth in search confirmations, with the revenue increase primarily attributed to higher average revenue per search. I treat the BLS output series as an upper-growth anchor but reduce the exact-industry constant-quality base because temporary help dominates the broader category and the company filing shows recent nominal growth was price-led rather than volume-led. The broad output forecast may misstate senior-hiring cycles in either direction, and one global firm may not represent boutique demand.
caveats: BLS publishes output for broader NAICS 561300, not executive search separately.
The recent public-company revenue increase was primarily price-driven and is not a constant-quality volume measure.
Executive-search demand is highly cyclical and concentrated in discretionary senior hiring.
d5.change_evidence: Exact 561312 constant-price receipts, retained-search confirmation counts across firms, average fee and search-duration data, executive vacancy rates, and customer insourcing rates would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Leadership turnover, succession, new-company formation, private-equity portfolio activity, and the difficulty of senior hiring sustain search demand, while macroeconomic contractions can pause executive mandates abruptly. AI may stimulate searches by changing required leadership skills, but it also equips internal talent teams to source more candidates directly.

caveats: No source measures eligibility within the injected lower-middle-market firm count.
A nominally qualifying firm may still be economically nontransferable if client relationships belong personally to one rainmaker.
The injected count is margin-bridged and may misclassify firms with unusually variable consultant compensation or owner distributions.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level Census product-line receipts or a commercial directory matched to ownership, external-client revenue, repeat-client share, employee count, and founder dependence for the supplied 360-firm population would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: The exact industry is defined around external executive-search and placement services, so most operating firms in the supplied lower-middle-market count fit the recurring outsourced-service lens. The discount covers firms dependent on a single founder, businesses dominated by one-off coaching or outplacement activity, captive or misclassified establishments, and operations without a transferable team, client book, or data asset.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 561312 ($5,623,299,414) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 561312 ($13,765,703,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[56]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $287,354 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 561312); class <5: 4,649 firms, avg revenue/firm $487,085 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $76,229 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 581 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,077,446 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $325,120 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 330 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,671,688 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $574,619 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 293 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,695,177 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,517,295 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 67 firms, avg revenue/firm $35,327,075 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,528,687 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 25 firms, avg revenue/firm $154,999,560 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $24,257,431 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 360

caveats: No direct source measures year-five operator necessity for executive search.
Internal talent teams may adopt the same sourcing tools as search firms and remove routine mandates.
Legal, privacy, and bias rules could preserve accountable intermediaries but also increase their compliance cost.
o.change_evidence: Longitudinal data on executive-search insourcing, software-only senior placements, client renewal after AI deployment, search failure and replacement rates, and the share of mandates retaining an external accountable adviser would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Clients and software can increasingly perform candidate discovery, résumé synthesis, outreach, and initial screening, but senior searches still require an accountable intermediary for confidential market access, off-limits management, reference triangulation, cultural and interpersonal assessment, persuasion of passive candidates, negotiation, and reputational responsibility. The operator-required share is lower for standardized or less confidential mandates and higher for board, chief-executive, and scarce-specialist searches.

q.bridge: Heidrick & Struggles describes retained executive-search fees of about one-third of estimated first-year compensation, generally billed in three increments and owed regardless of whether the client hires a candidate. I map that fixed, value-linked structure to relatively strong near-term retention of AI-enabled cost and capacity benefits, then discount over five years for procurement pressure, competitive fee negotiation, faster-search expectations, technology parity, consultant incentive sharing, and lower fees where clients self-source candidates. A large global retained-search firm may have greater pricing power than lower-middle-market boutiques, while specialized boutiques with scarce networks may retain more.
caveats: The observed billing structure comes from one large global retained-search firm.
No source measures sharing of AI productivity gains or the revenue mix of lower-middle-market firms.
Contingency-heavy firms may experience different retention and volume effects from retained firms.
q.change_evidence: A representative sample of boutique search contracts and invoices, segmented by retained versus contingency work and linked to post-AI fee changes, search duration, consultant compensation, and gross margin, would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Retainers tied to role compensation rather than documented labor hours do not mechanically pass lower delivery cost to clients, allowing productivity gains to appear as capacity or margin. Competitive renewal and the visibility of AI-enabled speed should transfer part of the benefit to clients over time.

rho.bridge: SHRM measures AI use by U.S. employer HR functions rather than executive-search providers: 51% report AI-supported recruiting, 89% of users report time or efficiency benefits, and 40% report planned expansion of recruiting AI over five years. I translate that customer-side adoption signal into faster provider adoption because search firms specialize in recruiting and have strong incentives to improve researcher and consultant throughput, then discount for smaller-firm integration capacity, confidential data, bias and employment-law exposure, hallucinations, proprietary-system migration, and mandatory human review. Employer HR adoption may understate specialist-provider intensity, but survey-reported use and benefits may overstate deeply integrated production coverage.
caveats: SHRM respondents are employer HR professionals, not executive-search firms.
Reported time savings do not quantify the share of exposed labor actually removed or redeployed.
Client confidentiality, algorithmic-bias controls, and fragmented legacy databases may slow smaller firms.
rho.change_evidence: A representative survey of U.S. executive-search firms measuring production workflow coverage, approved tool use, data-security controls, verified labor hours avoided, error rates, and adoption by firm size would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Candidate search, document generation, market mapping, note synthesis, and scheduling are already supported by available tools, and competitive pressure should spread implementation. Implementation will be slower in final assessment and client-facing decisions because errors can damage a placement, violate privacy expectations, or amplify bias.

s5.bridge: Gallup reports that 22% of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, while Heidrick & Struggles completed an exact-industry control merger in 2025. I haircut the cross-industry intention rate for noncompletion, family or internal transfers, founder-owned client relationships, partner departures, and transactions involving only teams or books of business; the observed large-company merger confirms buyer interest but does not justify raising the lower-middle-market probability. The general owner survey may overstate qualifying completions, while consolidation among specialist search boutiques may make the haircut too severe.
caveats: The Gallup statistic is an all-industry intention measure, not completed control transfers.
Heidrick & Struggles is far larger than the screened firms.
Team lift-outs and earn-in arrangements may not qualify as control transfers of durable operating firms.
s5.change_evidence: Closed U.S. executive-search transactions from a deal database, matched to firm size and ownership, plus broker outcomes and owner-age data for independent boutiques would materially change the range.
s5.rationale: Executive-search firms can transfer sector expertise, candidate data, brands, client relationships, and consultant teams, and the industry has demonstrated strategic and financial-buyer interest. Transferability is constrained by key-person dependence, client non-solicitation obligations, consultant mobility, and the ease with which producers can leave after a sale.

principal_driver: High-value retained mandates combine information-intensive delivery work with fees that are linked more to role value than to recorded labor hours.
principal_weakness: The most defensible service value and the most transferable enterprise value both depend heavily on individual consultants' relationships and judgment.
